sections.title: Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: BØRNS VILKÅR STOP SKIGT

section_path: Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The digital lives of children in Europe today are shaped by online platforms that offer both opportunity and risk. Therefore, Børns Vilkår urges EU policymakers to seize the Digital Fairness Act as an opportunity to build a better digital future for Europe's children. The evidence is overwhelming that without intervention, children will continue to be bombarded with manipulative ads, unsafe content feeds, and designs that put their well-being at risk and violate their rights and freedom.

section_path: Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: This position paper presents Børns Vilkår's recent research findings and policy recommendations aimed at strengthening children's rights and protections as digital consumers

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: Today's children face an enormous amount of commercial content and manipulative practices online. Social media, video platforms, and games have become finely tuned commercial machines applying deceptive techniques to manipulate choices, enhance data collection and challenging children and young people's ability to disengage with services due to strong marketing features. Børns Vilkår's recent analysis Forbrug i feed'et revealed how a young teenager's feed is an endless mix of entertainment and advertising where it is often difficult to tell ads apart from organic content. In our experiment with fictitious 13-year-old profiles, 46% of the posts viewed across TikTok, Instagram and YouTube had commercial character, including ads, influencer promotions, and brand posts (Børns Vilkår, Forbrug i feed'et, 2025), underlying the massive amount of commercial content presented to minors.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: Much of this content is far more targeted, effective, extensive and opaque than traditional marketing, making it hard for children to recognize the commercial intent and resist the pressure to consume. Indeed, many children themselves report being influenced: Two-thirds of Danish children say they use social media to get inspiration for things to buy and many specifically cite influencers as a key source of these purchase inspirations (Børns Vilkår, Forbrug i feed'et, 2025).

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: The cumulative "advertising pressure" on children is relentless – not limited to a TV commercial break or a billboard but following them into their bedrooms via personal devices. As children scroll through the mix of videos and ads, they are enticed by material desires: "The newest trend, the right shoes or a popular perfume – they want to be part of the community" as our report observes (Børns Vilkår, Forbrug i feed'et, 2025).

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: One in four children have felt pressured to buy a certain product to fit in with their peers, a figure that rises to one in three for girls. Social media amplifies these social pressures: In our study, 78% of children said they have at some point tried to get something they saw advertised online,

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: Børns Vilkår • Trekronergade 26 • 2500 Valby • Danmark • M +45 3555 5559

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: and one in seven children feel the urge to buy things from social media on a daily basis (Børns Vilkår, Forbrug i feed'et, 2025).

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
paragraphs.text: A particularly pervasive form of online commercial influence is influencer-marketing. Children and teens often look up to popular influencers as role models, blurring the line between genuine content and paid promotion. In a recent study, 77% of children in 7th grade reported watching influencer content, and many say they are inspired to buy the products influencers recommend or use, even when they rationally know it's advertising (Børns Vilkår, Børn og unges liv på sociale medier, 2024). There appears to be a cognitive dissonance for minors where they can rationalise the business model for influencers, yet believe that it is different when it concerns the influencers they follow.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In light of the above, Børns Vilkår urges EU policymakers to incorporate the following specific measures into the Digital Fairness Act. These recommendations aim to create an online environment that protects children from commercial exploitation:

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: **Limit Commercial Content and Hidden Marketing**: Impose strict limits on the volume and types of commercial content that children are exposed to. Marketing techniques that exploit children's inexperience such as influencer advertising without disclosure, or ads for unhealthy and age-inappropriate products should be banned. All permitted ads or sponsored content must be conspicuously labeled in child-friendly terms.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: **Age-Appropriate Design and Default Settings**: Require that any online service likely to be used by children (social media, games, apps, etc.) implement an "age-appropriate design" approach for known or likely child user such as: accounts set to private, location off, engagement-based recommender algorithms turned off by default, no profiling for content recommendations, and data minimization. This independent of whether an online service is defined as a VLOP or VLOSE.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: **Effective and Privacy-Friendly Age Verification**: Establish a European framework for robust, privacy-preserving age verification to help distinguish child and adult users online on all online services with commercial content. This could follow the current opportunities and definitions of age verification in the art. 28 guidelines of the DSA.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: To ensure that children under 15 are shielded from digital marketing of commercial products and services, platforms that do not implement effective and privacy-secure age verification must be free of all advertising and marketing content.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: **Prohibit Manipulative and Addictive Design Practices**: Define and ban specific exploitative design features that fuel excessive engagement or pressure spending by minors. The Digital Fairness Act should enumerate forbidden practices such as infinite scroll, autoplay mechanisms, loot boxes and overly gamified interfaces. The Act should empower regulators to update this list as new

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Børns Vilkår • Trekronergade 26 • 2500 Valby • Danmark • M +45 3555 5559 Side 2

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: manipulative techniques emerge. (For more information, see Forbrukerrådet, Commercial Exploitation of Children and Adolescents Online How to Ensure a Rights-Respecting Digital Childhood, 2024).

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Prohibit agencies and companies from using influencers to market commercial products or services to minors: Minors are heavily influenced by influencers, whom they often look up to as role models. This makes them more impressionable and less capable of recognizing when influencer content is in fact advertising. Monitoring influencer marketing is a complex and resource-intensive task for society, and the rules can be difficult for individual influencers to navigate. Therefore, the prohibition should target companies and influencer agencies, which must ensure that influencers always label commercial content and that such content is not made accessible to underage users.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Children's images and personal data must never be used for product development or AI training unless explicit, informed consent has been granted by both the child and their parent or legal guardian: In recent years, major technology companies have begun collecting user-generated photos, videos, and other personal data from platforms to train artificial intelligence systems capable of generating new images and content. This includes accessing private images on user's devices. Once incorporated into large AI models, children's likeness may be reproduced, manipulated, or repurposed in new and potentially harmful contexts beyond the child's or family's control. This is a violation of children's rights and should be made illegal.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: AI chatbots must never use data from minors for commercial purposes: AI-driven chatbots are designed to build a sense of trust, empathy, and loyalty with users. A dynamic that can easily lead minors to share private or sensitive information. The past years, the increase in AI chatbots – especially sychophantic – has increased the challenges for users to distinguish between chatbots and humans. Furthermore, data is collected and applied for commercial practices. Therefore, all collection or processing of minors' data for advertising, profiling, or product optimization must be strictly prohibited.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Human-like or sycophantic AI chatbots must not be accessible to minors: AI systems deliberately designed to simulate human companionship, emotional responsiveness, or flattery can create powerful psychological bonds, especially among vulnerable users. Their design should never exploit minor's need for attention, comfort, or belonging, and can distort their natural development of social competencies. Where chatbots are available to younger users (e.g. educational or informational systems), they must operate under clear ethical safeguards such as

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Børns Vilkår • Trekronergade 26 • 2500 Valby • Danmark • M +45 3555 5559 Side 3

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: being transparent about being non-human, non-commercial, and free of manipulative features. Commercial sycophantic or human-like chatbots marketed as 'friends', 'boyfriends/girlfriends' or 'companions' should not be accessible to minors and be required to implement strict age verification methods.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
Børns Vilkår's Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Stronger Enforcement and Accountability: The success of the Digital Fairness Act will depend on enforcement. We call for strengthened roles and resources for consumer protection and digital services regulators to monitor compliance. The Act should establish clear liability for non-compliance, including significant fines for platforms or companies that do not comply. We also recommend that the Commission issue binding guidelines or codes of conduct under the DFA to guide industry compliance (similar to the DSA guidelines) and involving both children and child rights experts in their development.

section_path: The Commercial Pressure on Children Online
